title: Third-party politics
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on third parties and their candidates in the US, and the structural barriers they face

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements accurately summarizes why it is virtually impossible for third-party candidates to become president?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Electoral College uses a winner-take-all system
Third parties do not qualify for super PAC funds in presidential races
Third-party candidates pay exorbitant fees to have their names printed on general election ballots in most states
Primary elections weed out strong third-party candidates

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an important structural barrier to third-party and independent candidate success in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Winner-take-all districts
Multiple-member districts
Platform incorporation by major parties
Proportional representation

assessment_items.question_content:
**Highest vote shares for independent and third-party US Senate candidates in a regular November election while opposed by both a Democrat and a Republican**

Year | Candidate | Third Party| State | Vote (%) | Result | Margin
- | - | - | -| -| -
**1940** | La Follette Jr. (i) | Progressive |WI | 45.3% | W | +3.9%
**1940** | Lemke | Independent |ND | 35.1% | L | -3.0%
**1970** | Byrd Jr. (i) | Independent |VA | 53.5% | W | +22.3%
**1970** | Buckley  | Conservative |NY | 38.8% | W | +2.0%
**2006** | Lieberman (i) | Independent |CT | 49.7% | W | +10.0%
**2010** | Murkowski (i)  | Write-in |AK | 39.5% | W | +4.0%
**2012** | King | Independent |ME | 52.9% | W | +22.1%

***Note:** "(i)" denotes an incumbent*

Source: "[The Low Success Rate of Independent and Third-Party Candidates in Senate Elections](http://www.centerforpolitics.org/crystalball/articles/the-low-success-rate-of-independent-and-third-party-candidates-in-u-s-senate-elections/)"

**Based on the table above, which of the following variables made the most difference in winning an election for a third-party or independent candidate in these 7 elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Whether the candidate was an incumbent
In which decade they ran
Which party they represented
Which state they were from

assessment_items.question_content:
**Highest vote shares for independent and third-party US Senate Candidates in a regular November Election while opposed by both a Democrat and a Republican**

Year | Candidate | Third Party| State | Vote (%) | Result | Margin
- | - | - | -| -| -
**1940** | La Follette Jr. (i) | Progressive |WI | 45.3% | W | +3.9%
**1940** | Lemke | Independent |ND | 35.1% | L | -3.0%
**1970** | Byrd Jr. (i) | Independent |VA | 53.5% | W | +22.3%
**1970** | Buckley | Conservative |NY | 38.8% | W | +2.0%
**2006** | Lieberman (i) | Independent |CT | 49.7% | W | +10.0%
**2010** | Murkowski (i)  | Write-in |AK | 39.5% | W | +4.0%
**2012** | King | Independent |ME | 52.9% | W | +22.1%

***Note:** "(i)" denotes an incumbent*

Source: "[The Low Success Rate of Independent and Third-Party Candidates in Senate Elections](http://www.centerforpolitics.org/crystalball/articles/the-low-success-rate-of-independent-and-third-party-candidates-in-u-s-senate-elections/)"

**Based on the table above, which of the following is an important conclusion that a third-party candidate could make when running for a Senate seat against both Republican and Democrat opponents?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They need to acquire at least 38% of the vote to win a contested election
They need to run as a member of the Progressive Party
They need to run as a Conservative to be a viable third-party candidate
They need to run in the northeastern part of the country

assessment_items.question_content:
Candidate | Party | Popular Votes | Percentage of popular votes | Electoral votes | Percentage of Electoral
- | - | -
William J. Clinton | Democratic | $44,908,254$ | $43\%$ | $370$| $69\%$
George H.W. Bush | Republican | $39,102, 243$ | $37\%$ | $168$ | $31\%$
Ross Perot | Independent | $19,743,821$ | $19\%$ | $0$ | $0\%$

>-Source: [The American Presidency Project](http://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/showelection.php?year=1992)

**Based on the data shown in the table and your knowledge about the Electoral College, which of the following statements best explains the distribution of electoral votes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Electoral votes are awarded to the candidate who wins the higher percentage of votes within a state
Electoral votes are awarded based on the proportion of votes won within a state
Electoral votes are awarded to the candidate who wins a majority of the votes within a state
Electoral votes are only awarded to a candidate if there is a clear majority winner, or the election goes to a runoff

assessment_items.question_content:
Candidate | Party | Popular Votes | Percentage of popular votes | Electoral votes | Percentage of Electoral
- | - | -
William J. Clinton | Democratic | $44,908,254$ | $43\%$ | $370$| $69\%$
George H.W. Bush | Republican | $39,102, 243$ | $37\%$ | $168$ | $31\%$
Ross Perot | Independent | $19,743,821$ | $19\%$ | $0$ | $0\%$

>-Source: [The American Presidency Project](http://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/showelection.php?year=1992)

**Which of the following statements describes why Ross Perot won zero electoral votes in the 1992 presidential election?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The winner-take-all system awarded electoral votes to the candidate who won the most votes in that state
The proportional representation system awarded him the same percentage of electoral votes as what he won in the popular vote
The winner-take-all system forced a runoff between the top two candidates, and Ross Perot was the third candidate
The proportional representation system only awarded electoral votes to the top two candidates who received votes

assessment_items.question_content:
The Green Party (GPUS) advocates environmentalism, social justice, and anti-war/anti-racism agendas.

**Which of the following statements accurately describes a barrier to the Green Party winning congressional seats or the presidency?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Incorporation of third-party issue positions or platforms by major political parties
Third parties are unable to qualify for super PAC funds in federal elections
State election boards banning third-party access to general election ballots
Implied prohibition against third-party organizations in Article I of the Constitution

assessment_items.question_content:
Like the old Progressive Party, a new generation third-party called the Working Families Party (WFP) has successfully competed in specific geographies in recent years, forging opportunistic alliances with either of the major parties while building a precinct-level organization and support. 

**Based on this information and current election rules, which of the following is the best strategy for the WFP to influence public policy in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Concentrate on “grass roots” campaigns in local elections lacking two-party competition
Run candidates in national elections where they can serve as the spoiler
Challenge the nominees of one or both major parties at their national conventions
Run against both Republican and Democratic candidates in high-profile state or local elections

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how third parties affect the US political system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They can shift national attention to particular issues and influence the outcome of elections
They can restructure state governments to be more responsive to the electorate
They can pass legislation protecting the middle class
They can win crucial elections at the national level given the right candidates

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements is the most valid criticism of voting for a third party in the US political system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Your third-party vote may help a candidate that you disagree with
You may be aiding a foreign government when you lend support to lesser known parties
Third parties cannot change the amount of political corruption in Washington DC
Third parties cannot make a difference at any level of government

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7fafba851f94de3fce9ce06c2652639a.jpg)

>-Source: In 1896, the Populist Party was a major third party looking to support a presidential candidate.  [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bryan,_Judge_magazine,_1896.jpg)

**Which of the following describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A risk of incorporating a third party's agenda is that the third party's platform may eclipse the dominant party's platform
Incorporation of a third-party’s agenda is a way for a dominant party to secure voters who would have voted for the third-party
The major political parties are unwilling to incorporate a third-party agenda because third parties are often unsuccessful
Third parties are more successful than major parties because they appeal to a broader base of voters

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of a way that one of the major political parties can negatively impact a third party’s chance of winning? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Add issues that concern third-party voters into their platforms
Appeal to the Electoral College to keep them off ballots
Attempt to form a coalition with the third party
Petition state government to become multi-member districts
